text: With the rapid advancement of robotic surgery, there is a need to develop a robotic surgical education assessment and training tool similar to FLS for laparoscopic surgical training. The objective of this video is to develop and test an all-in-one psychomotor skills prototype for possible use in development of the fundamentals of robotic surgery curriculum. The prototype device in this video was built with simple household materials including an eight inch styrofoam ball cut in half, felt material, foam shapes, wire, magnet, hair ties, puff paint, shirt button, and a latex swimming cap.

The simulation setup features a dome prototype placed inside a robotic docking model. Visible instruments include the bipolar Maryland dissector, monopolar curved scissors, needle driver, and other tools used for evaluating various robotic surgical skills.

The learner docks the robotic system and inserts instruments, ensuring all are visible in the field of view. The learner then performs the ring tower transfer task, picking up a ring from the middle tower with one robotic arm, transferring it to the other arm, and placing it on the side tower without touching the wire, demonstrating end-to-wrist manipulation and hand-eye coordination.

The learner performs a surgeon's knot, ensuring the eyelets touch, followed by one throw. Next, horizontal mattress suturing is demonstrated through target points using an ovicle, with a button affixed at the beginning of the suture.

The learner uses one robotic arm to pick up a simulated vein and another arm for retraction, then switches to the monopolar scissors to cut the vein at marked locations. In the clover leaf dissection task, the learner carefully cuts a pattern on the latex skin layer without damaging the colored felt material underneath, demonstrating precise cutting and tissue handling skills.

The learner retracts the cut-out flap using the fourth robotic arm, exposing the underlying tissue. The vessel is dissected and cauterized with a bipolar instrument, followed by cutting with monopolar scissors. The exercise demonstrates manipulation, cutting, and dissection at various angles, utilizing multiple robotic arms for exposure and retraction.

These next few pictures demonstrate the model after completion of all exercises. The total time for a novice to intermediate learner to complete all exercises was fifteen minutes. This all-in-one model is reusable and only the skin of the dome needs to be removed and replaced with a new skin for the next learner. Also, magnets were used to allow for easy placement and removal of the cones. The total cost for this model was approximately fifteen dollars. Although some improvements and adjustments are to be applied to future models, this video demonstrates the feasibility of creating an all-in-one economic psychomotor skills model for robotic surgery training.
